speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape two of the interview with Tola Hauptman. Tola, you were talking about your father considering going to Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And he went with his brother-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and then-- he-- he had two brothers, with two brothers. And because the youngest brother wife was from Brest, which was close to the Russian frontier, she went too with her children. My father couldn't stay. He was only a few days, and he couldn't be separated from his family. He knew that he's the strong one and he will-- in-- in a sort of a danger, he will help us. So he came back, leaving behind the middle brother and the younger brother with his family, and we never heard about them anymore. The middle brother, the Max, came back after some time to-- decided to come back. But before he-- he came back, it was still the bombardment was going on. And they were-- you see, they were partnered to all the business, and they had a beautiful apartment in [INAUDIBLE] which was a main street in Warsaw. It was a center, and it was opposite the station, you see. And that brother Max asked my father to go to Warsaw to look after his family.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was really silly of us to go there because the bombardment was mostly in the center, you see, and especially on that main station, you know? Central station. But my father being so-- such a family man, and he loved all his family, we went there. And luckily we survived. It wasn't long. It was about months or so, the-- the war, you know? So you were there until?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until--

speaker: interviewer
text: What date?

speaker: interviewee
text: They came in, and I saw them marching, and it was terrible. There were thousand of people on both side of the street. And first came the-- the Germans on the motorcycles and then the others. And we were watching. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened after they came in? What happened to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went back to the-- to our own home in [? Krakow. ?] Yes. We were there until-- oh, in-- in between, they were looking for my father because someone-- we were already living in [? Krakow, ?] in Warsaw, sort of in the suburb, but my father was very well known in Wawel where we lived before. And someone killed a German, and that was the first atrocity what they did that was in Wawel was they killed 110 or 120 people. They were looking mostly for the Jews, but they also killed a lot of Poles. And my father was the first of the list-- on the list to get him but someone from the Poles warned him, and he went into hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he left you and your mother at that stage and your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah. And actually, the Germans came and looked for him, but he wasn't there. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father continue to work at all after the Germans came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at all. Not at all. I remember, matter of fact, before we went to the ghetto, it was on the second floor from us was a gynecologist of Dr. Ladyzinski. Very, very nice man. He married late and his wife was a lovely woman. And my mother got pregnant and--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was making the abortion, yes. That was during that, in the '40s.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the '40s?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Without any anesthetic, without anything that I recall too. And then we met-- my father met a very big industrialist who was a owner of [INAUDIBLE]. That was the shaving sort of Gilettes. Very-- he was a multimillionaire. And he was actually German, but he live in Poland. And his wife was German, non-Jewish. And the German wife was most agitated, and she was telling him to leave because she knew that it will be bad. So they-- they had-- they had a car, and they also had a-- a few trucks, and he said to my father, we can go towards Russia and over there we'll see.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father, of course, he was grateful and he agreed. And my mother [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] wasn't so very keen on it, and we started to cry. It was cold sort of and at night, and my mother said I'm not going. So we took our belongings and went back to our flat. Was this after your father had gone into hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was a question of a few days because after they killed the 100 odd people, I remember going there and seeing the dead people. It was a distant cousin, and a lot of people couldn't recognize really. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went and looked at the bodies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were they? Where were the bodies?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Wawel. That was in Wawel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they in an open area?

speaker: interviewee
text: A sort of open area, yes. One or-- or two was sort of buried with the dead bodies, so one or two survive. But it was about 100, 120 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So what happened next to you and your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So we were living on-- in [? Krakow ?] until we went to the ghetto. That was about 15 of November, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that-- and what year was that? The 15th of November--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We had a-- I had a aunt who was living before the ghetto in-- in that area, and she had two rooms. And she-- they gave one room to us. And I can recall that my father had typhoid and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that before you got to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that before you got--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. In [NON-ENGLISH], in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because there-- there were lice everywhere. And we were-- my mother found one or two lice, you know? Also, we were walking in the middle of the road, but there were people everywhere, couldn't avoid it. So he was very sick and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Jewish police came to take him to the Umschlagplatz. They had-- they had names. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So what year was this? Was this straight away when you entered the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I-- no, not straight away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, let's-- let's go--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, slowly.

speaker: interviewer
text: --back a bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was going to school at that time. It was-- the school was in hiding, you see? And we were given-- my father used to built sort of houses for the colonies like for the train workers or tram-- tramway or officers. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this in the ghetto? That's what he did--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. No, it was before. Why I tell you about it, because one of the Poles who was working on the trains-- the-- not a conductor. The mechanic or something, he used to come. He had a-- a pass to go into the ghetto, and he used to come and bring us food. That was-- he was a wonderful man until they caught-- it was a few times-- until they caught the food on him and broke his front teeth. He couldn't come anymore. But he at the time when he came, the last time, my father was sick. And that could've been '41, something like that. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Well, just before also your father got sick, you were saying that you were going to school in the ghetto in hiding.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes it was in hiding, yes. It was-- The school was-- every time in a-- in a student's sort of place and every time a different place so the German or the police wouldn't find it. It was illegal.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long-- And how long did that go on for?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] That was one and a half years, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it went on from 1940 when you came to the middle of 1941?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah. And then with the-- with the action, with the sort of when the liquidation started, so it was no use, and-- and everyone has to hide somewhere. And people were afraid to do anything, you know? It was just a matter of surviving and to have enough to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Well, let's go back to when you arrived in the ghetto. Tell us a bit about the conditions in the ghetto for you and your-- for you--

speaker: interviewee
text: The conditions were appalling. We were lucky to have that one room, but then when the conditions were worse, that was this is mine and this is yours. And my father started to look for something on our own. And he found a room-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a-- a big kitchen and a small room in Zamenhof Street. That was in-- in the big ghetto. And it was very lucky because next door was a bakery so the whole wall was warm. We didn't need any-- anything to warm us up.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when the action started, the Germans started to-- to take people to Umschlagplatz, you know, then from there by train to concentration camps. My father discovered that there is that one little room can be a hiding place from us-- for us because that was I recall it was a enormous antique wardrobe. And my father took the inner wall out and put some screws, you know? He knew about it, and he tie it up from inside. So in other words, when we went there he put the-- the missing part, it-- nothing was visible. And the frame of the door was lower than the wardrobe so no one could even suggest that there is another room. Also what was lucky, it was a coincident that in that little room there was a window high up. It was sort of half of the window, and they couldn't see us and it was going to the next number. Like this was number nine, so that was 11, you see? So it was a perfect hiding place. And people every time it was action, you know, people were hiding anywhere, anywhere. And mostly they were running up, up to these-- you know, under the roof. But the Germans and the police always found them, you see? And what else was there? My father had a [NON-ENGLISH]. What you-- you know? For the wood. To break the wood. How do you-- An axe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Axe. He had an axe, and it was like he would be in a trance. He was working on that axe. He was sharpening it up all the time. We were petrified, and we didn't want to stay there. And my father was so energetic. He said, you not only stay, you lie flat. And whoever will come will be killed no matter who-- German or police, Polish police or Jewish police. No one will come out alive from this place. That what he did. And this was the way we survive until that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long were you in that-- in that room for with that sort of protection to hide?

speaker: interviewee
text: As long as-- as long as the actions were. And then we had to move again because they said-- ah, my father went out after that action, and he saw there are no people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was in preparation-- So that was in preparation for the one action?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This was the first-- the first or the second, I don't remember. But anyway from the next building, the number 11, where the window was overlooking, someone yelled from the last floor, we've got a invalid here-- to the Germans in German because they were mostly-- there were a lot of Germans who were brought to the ghetto and they were so stupid. They were from second generations already--

speaker: interviewer
text: They were German Jews

speaker: interviewee
text: German Jews. And they said, Hitler won't do anything to us. We've got sons in the army, because it was to the-- you know, they-- they were second-generation Jews, and they believed so. So whatever the German said, they agreed to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one German Jew from the fifth floor was yelling, I've got a invalid here. What to do with-- with-- with him or with her? Bring her down. Nothing will happen to-- to her, you know? So my father said, you've got a proof they're going to kill us all. That was the proof. And then we believe our father because we didn't sort of-- we couldn't understand. So that was the big action? Do you remember what year that was?

words.text: Do you remember what year that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was enormous action. It was-- they took-- only one or two people were still alive hiding somewhere, but they took all of them. What I recall also from that period of time--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what year it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was '41. It was still '41, yes. I recall [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I couldn't walk on the street. I-- I just avoided it, but when I walk, I-- you couldn't-- you couldn't miss it. Every few steps it was a dead body. And they were just skin and bones. Some of them, the whole families were-- you know, I-- I didn't know what it is. They were enormous. They were all swollen. I can recall a beautiful symphony orchestra, about six people playing beautiful from one backyard to another, going and asking for-- for some food.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they-- was this in the ghetto you're talking-- In the ghetto, yes. And they were all swollen and they were-- in your eyes they were dying. I never forget one, suppose it was a young man. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was shocking. He was leaking from the-- on the road where he was, the sputum. Can you imagine that? He was so hungry and thirsty.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- most of the time, I was-- I felt sick. You know, we-- luckily, my-- my father was a good provider, so he brought what he brought. It wasn't any much clothing but mostly food. He was a terrific provider.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- we were starving, but we couldn't die from it, because it was set every day two pieces of bread, a bit of marmalade, and he even had chocolate he didn't give us. Finally, when he-- we had to move and he-- he found a-- a pusher, sort of a pusher from a market or something, and he was pushing it. I notice that we had a lot of food left, like, you know, jams and-- and cocoa and chocolate. So he was rationing it?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was rationing. He didn't want us to eat because he expected that the war will last, you know? So we were always hungry.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after that action and you had to leave the place you were in, is that when your father got together the trolley with all the food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go then?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to the-- that was called-- you see, they were some entrepreneurs, German entrepreneurs, who were-- to Schilling Shop, it was called. Schilling Shop. It was a-- a timber yard.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my father, as a member of the-- how do you call it? On the trade, they took him in. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and he hide the whole family. Because we have to leave all the flats. Nobody was there, you know? Mostly people were, you know, in concentration camps already.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we-- we belonged to a smaller group. It was always sort of smaller and smaller, and my father said, I will hide-- you will hide under the timber. And that what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went to the--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were sleeping on the-- I-- I remember, on the timber. And the timber had, I don't know how you call. It doesn't exist here. It was a-- they were insects, were biting terrible during the night. I was scratching myself. I couldn't sleep. And luckily it was warm, still warm.

speaker: interviewer
text: So still it was, the German were coming. There was action from time to time. And my father, you know, he could predict what will happen. So it was sort of a [NON-ENGLISH]. It's a hut, you see, in the timber yard. And it was from old, very old timber. And somehow he found, you know, the same timber, old one, and he put double wall.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were-- my intermediate family and some other people who notice it, and they pushed themselves in. And it was a woman with a child. It was a baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who were the people? You all hid there, did you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather, my-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my cousins, my uncle, my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My father was--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, your brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother, yes. My-- my father was sort of responsible for the whole shop to-- attendant sort of, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: That was his work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That everything is going smoothly, that everything is all right, that there are no strangers. And, of course, this is the certificate I got from Yad Vashem, where it's said that my father was hiding people under the timber. And it was one woman with a child, and the child started to cry.

speaker: interviewer
text: With you? The child that was with-- you said there was--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That-- we were in a hut. They were under the [INAUDIBLE] of-- you know, under the wood, hidden. And if not that child who started to cry, you know, everything would go smoothly, but they found-- they found them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the Germans came, they heard the baby crying?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Exactly. And we were petrified because in our hiding place, it was a baby, maybe a few months. And my grandfather said, look, you have to put your hand on mouth because otherwise we all gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: And luckily, the-- the child was crying, but when the German-- when the German came, they had a sort of the-- a long-- how do you call that? Not a revolver, but they were picking into the wall because they suspect that it could be double wall, you know? And luckily, they did not assess, you know? Was it a knife on the end of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's where--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Very, very sharp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they didn't catch anybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because they were doing it on the other side, not on-- on our side. In the meantime, the child started to cry, so they all ran over there. But my father was a victim, because he was responsible, and they started to beat him up. And maybe it was a few minutes, and my mother knew about it. And my mother said, I'm going. I can't stand it. They beating him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my grandfather said, no, it's not him-- because he wanted to save us, you know? It was hard to hold my-- my mother quiet, you know? And they were beating with like horses [NON-ENGLISH], yes? You know? They were beating in one direction in that eye. You know, all the time, maybe 12 times or I-- I can't recall.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father had blood everywhere coming, and they took him-- and also I don't know whether he lost I think some front teeth. And they took him to Umschlagplatz from where it was already late. At that stage no one could get out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was--

speaker: interviewee
text: From Umschlagplatz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at Umschlagplatz is where they-- they took people to where from Umschlagplatz?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- I tell you what it was. It was a station.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, and from-- what did they do at the-- once they got the people at that station?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they kept thousands of people next to each other until the-- the cattle trains came. And they put the people in and took them to-- to their destination.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Well, we'll just leave it there, and we'll finish this tape now and continue on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Alright.
